_provenance: fan-out:wf_d565ece0 (haiku research + sonnet verify + sonnet score)
name: Hylas 54

facts.value: Solid lead external ballast, 20,000-20,020 lbs; ballast-to-displacement ratio 43%

sources.title: Hylas 54 RS - Sailing Magazine
sources.quote: External lead ballast weighing just over 20,000 pounds, creating a ballast-to-displacement ratio of 43

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Ballast: 20,020 lbs

facts.value: Not publicly available in specifications; stability characteristics emphasize form stability via beam aft design, fine bow entry, and rocker

sources.title: Hylas 54 Review - Sail Whisperer
sources.quote: Beam aft design providing stability and interior space; rocker under hull reduces pitching motion

facts.value: STIX value not specified in available documentation; yacht rated as very stable/stiff with excellent righting capability if capsized

sources.title: Sailboat Guide - Hylas 54
sources.quote: Very stable/stiff boat with excellent righting capability if capsized; best suited for bluewater cruising

facts.value: CE Category A/B (exact category unclear); only 2 of production units delivered to EU with CE approval; designed as offshore/ocean cruiser

sources.title: Hylas 54 - sailboatdata
sources.quote: Extremely rare to market with CE-approval, with only two originally supplied into EU market with CE-approval

facts.value: Not factory standard; Webasto hydronic heating systems commonly added as owner upgrade; reverse cycle air conditioning available (16,000 BTU saloon, 11,000 BTU each cabin)

sources.title: Hylas 54 - sailboatdata
sources.quote: Owners have added hydronic Webasto heating systems that complement reverse cycle air conditioning

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Cruisair 220vAC reverse-cycle air-conditioning system: 16,000BTU unit in saloon, 11,000BTU in aft cabin and forward cabin

facts.value: Balsa-cored GRP deck and superstructure for heat/noise insulation; solid GRP laminate under deck fittings and high-stress areas; Heat Shield Insulation between deck and headliner on some models

sources.title: Hylas 54 - sailboatdata
sources.quote: Balsa-cored GRP (fiberglass) deck and superstructure with coring for greater insulation against heat and noise; solid GRP laminate beneath deck fittings

facts.value: Keel-stepped aluminum mast available as option; triple spreader Selden mast standard, most commonly deck-stepped

sources.title: Hylas 54 - David Walters Yachts
sources.quote: Can be keel-stepped aluminum mast with triple spreaders

facts.value: Watertight collision bulkhead forward with independent overboard drainage; watertight stern sections across aft lazarettes and stern locker with integral swim platform

sources.title: Hylas 54 - David Walters Yachts
sources.quote: Watertight collision bulkhead forward with independent overboard drainage; watertight stern sections across aft lazarettes

facts.value: No interior helm; center cockpit exposed design is standard. Cockpit is comfortable, well-thought-out, secure in blow with U-shaped seating and pedestal instrument panel access

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Spacious cockpit contains U shaped seating with comfortable seat and backrest cushions; well-thought-out and secure in a blow

facts.value: Double walk-through passageway offering optimal ventilation and movement throughout interior; master stateroom includes outboard portlights for ventilation; raised saloon allows better airflow

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Double walk through passageway offering optimal ventilation and movement throughout the interior; master stateroom includes outboard portlights

facts.value: Raised saloon version provides additional natural light but increases solar heat gain; cockpit has teak varnished table but no explicit bimini/shade fabric mentioned in standard configuration

sources.title: Hylas 54 Review: New Evolution - Jordan Yacht Brokerage
sources.quote: Raised saloon allows more natural light into the main saloon

facts.value: Twin FrigoBoat DC Freezer/Refrigeration systems with keel coolers and thermostatic controls; Sea Frost 12V refrigeration also found on some examples (air or water cooled)

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Large twin FrigoBoat DC Freezer/Refrigeration systems with keel coolers, thermostatic controls and temperature gauges

facts.value: Spectra 400 watermaker with remote control panel at navigation station common; capacity varies by specific yacht configuration

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Spectra 400 watermaker with remote control panel at nav station

facts.value: Multiple deckhouse vents, cabin portholes, and strategic opening ports throughout; raised saloon aids natural convection; no forced air/HVAC standard (reverse-cycle AC available as upgrade)

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Master stateroom includes outboard portlights for ventilation; double walk through passageway offering optimal ventilation

facts.value: Not factory-equipped; Hydrovane, Aries Vane Gear, and Windpilot are aftermarket options used on comparable 54' bluewater cruisers

sources.title: Windvane Self-Steering Guide - Practical Boat Owner
sources.quote: Popular windvane brands: Hydrovane, Aries Vane Gear, Windpilot for offshore sailing

facts.value: Various autopilot systems installed post-production; electric winches on deck enable easy helm access for shorthanded operation

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Review! - Rover's Blog
sources.quote: Owner set up boat for shorthanded sailing; electric winches positioned for easy helm access

facts.value: House batteries: three 700 Ah 8D units standard (some newer refits: lithium 4x330Ah Victron LiFePO4); Northern Lights 6kW generator common; solar arrays (4x180W typical on refits); wind generators possible

sources.title: Hylas 54 RS - Sailing Magazine
sources.quote: House Batteries: Three 700-amp-hour 8D units

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: NEW (2025) Northern Lights 6kW generator with sound shield; (4) 180W Solar panels on stern tower

facts.value: Large beam aft toward transom (15'9" beam); considerable carry of beam aft provides stable platform and voluminous interior; integral swim platform with transom steps standard

sources.title: Hylas 54 Specifications
sources.quote: Beam: 15 ft 9 in

sources.title: Hylas 54 Review: New Evolution - Jordan Yacht Brokerage
sources.quote: Large beam on Hylas 54 is well aft, resulting in stable platform and voluminous interior

facts.value: Freeboard not specified in standard specs; raised saloon option increases headroom and light penetration vs. standard version; design provides good visibility from wheel unobstructed

sources.title: Hylas 54 Review: New Evolution - Jordan Yacht Brokerage
sources.quote: Raised saloon allows more natural light into the main saloon with slightly taller windows; visibility from wheel is unobstructed

facts.value: Wide side decks for safe foredeck work; 32-inch stanchions and double lifelines standard; molded nonskid not aggressive (good for barefoot but can be slick when wet)

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Review! - Rover's Blog
sources.quote: Side decks are wide; stanchions 32-inch with double lifelines; molded nonskid is not aggressive

facts.value: Extended stern pushpit as optional feature aids recovery; integral skeg (vs. spade rudder) prevents flotsam damage; one example (Acadia) retrofitted with FLIR thermal + Doppler radar for MOB tracking

sources.title: Hylas 54 For Sale - ACADIA - Hylas Yacht Collection
sources.quote: Mast mounted FLIR Thermal Imaging Camera integrates with Acadia's Doppler Radar for MOB events with thermal imaging

sources.title: Hylas 56 Review - Cruising Compass
sources.quote: Integral skeg (vs spade rudder) prevents flotsam damage underway and grounding damage

facts.value: Delivery skipper with 40,000 miles on various Hylas models weathered Hurricanes Bob, Grace, Mitch attesting to solid construction and seaworthiness; Can beam reach in 35 knots under staysail and main

sources.title: Hylas 54 RS - Sailing Magazine
sources.quote: Delivery skipper with nearly 40,000 miles aboard different Hylas models, enduring bouts with Hurricanes Bob, Grace and Mitch

facts.value: Fixed staysail standard cutter rig with Selden triple spreader mast; cutter staysail provides versatility in wind range; fixed staysail much more often used than if needing manual deployment

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Review! - Rover's Blog
sources.quote: Fixed staysail proved valuable—much more often than we would if we needed to pull it out

facts.value: Recommend inspection every 10-15 years; similar 44' boats require ~$10,000-$15,000 for rod rigging replacement including labor; typical age now (2026) is 16-27 years, most likely due for rerig

sources.title: Cost for Standing Rigging Replacement
sources.quote: 44-foot boat costs approximately $10,000 for steel rigging, including installation; J35 estimated $10,000–$15,000 for rod rigging replacement

facts.value: Rudder foam-filling failure during 2005 Caribbean 1500: two 54s lost lower third of rudder under high stress (linked to prior groundings); steering can feel stiff (noted in owner forums); turbo lag on Yammer engine requires full throttle 30 seconds for docking

sources.title: Hylas 54 Marine Survey Common Findings
sources.quote: Two 54 Hylases lost substantial portions of rudders in 2005 Caribbean 1500; lower third was foam unattached to partial skeg

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Review! - Rover's Blog
sources.quote: Engine turbo lag requires throttle pinned at full for as long as 30 seconds before developing power

facts.value: Yammer (Yancer) diesel parts availability is challenging; newer components no longer in production; some owners wait 3+ weeks for parts from Japan; Hylas-specific parts moderate availability; Selden mast/rigging parts reasonable; no longer in production (last units ~2010)

sources.title: Hylas 54 Parts Availability - HarborMoor
sources.quote: Yammer (Yancer) no longer offers certain pumps for sale, despite many boats equipped with engine

sources.title: Hylas 54 Owners Forum - SailNet
sources.quote: High relative expense of parts, low relative availability of parts, and lack of qualified mechanics with experience

praise: Spacious, comfortable, capable, fast—consistently praised for performance and livability balance
Exceptional build quality with solid wood joinery, through-bolted fittings, meticulous machinery organization
German Frers design with excellent sail plan enabling 200+ nautical mile days
Strong form stability via wide transom, fine bow entry, and rocker; excellent handling characteristics
Well-earned reputation for strength and ability to withstand rough sea conditions
Owner testimonial: Bill Regan (Sapphire) noted 'half the price of Oyster 53' while achieving +200-mile sailing days consistently
Raised saloon version gains 3:1 market preference for additional light and space
Shorthanded sailing capability with thoughtful sail lead design and electric winches
One-owner boats show exceptional pride; design innovation by original owners evident years after resale
complaints: Engine turbo lag on Yammer 125hp requires 30 seconds full throttle for docking precision
Inadequate original battery configuration (single battery for bow thruster and windlass); later models fixed this
Mooring cleat positioning too far outboard prevents fair-lead snubber routing
Chain rollers cause aggressive chafing on anchor warp (owners add mini bridles to mitigate)
Underperforms in light air under 8 knots; 'not particularly rewarding to daysail'
Molded nonskid not aggressive (slick when wet)
Center cockpit design allows less leg room at helm vs. aft-cockpit alternatives
Original steering can feel stiff (noted in Hylas forums June 2026)
Yammer diesel parts availability poor, especially older components
known_issues: Rudder foam-fill failure documented: two 54s lost lower third of rudder during 2005 Caribbean 1500 (linked to prior groundings); indicates potential structural vulnerability under extreme stress
Standing rigging likely due for replacement on all extant boats (built 1999-2010, now 16-27 years old)
Yammer diesel: parts no longer in production; long lead times (3+ weeks) for replacements from Japan
Limited availability of qualified Hylas mechanics, especially outside USA
Higher relative cost of replacement parts and spares vs. mass-production cruisers

forums.title: Hylas Yacht Owners Association Forum
forums.quote: 5,652 posts in 1,236 topics by 872 members; unofficial but active community; recent post 'Steering stiff' suggests ongoing dialogue on maintenance and handling

forums.title: Cruisers Forum - Hylas Discussions
forums.quote: Multiple threads on sail selection, centerboard options, and cruising advice; active Hylas List Serve participants

forums.title: YBW Forum - Liveaboard Cruising
forums.quote: Hylas 54 mentioned as comparison for long-distance cruising boats 49-55 feet; supported by Hylas owner community

forums.title: Rover's Blog - Hylas 54/56 Owner Runbook
forums.quote: In-depth owner documentation with 7,000+ passage miles experience; practical maintenance and operational tips

refit_items.action: Full replacement (all units now 16-27 years old; recommend 10-15 year cycle)

sources.title: Standing Rigging Replacement Cost
sources.quote: 44-foot boat ~$10,000; comparable 35' J35 $10,000–$15,000 including labor and mast removal

refit_items.action: Install Webasto hydronic heating (not factory standard; essential for northern cruising)

sources.title: Hylas 54 - sailboatdata
sources.quote: Owners have added hydronic Webasto heating systems

refit_items.action: Overhaul or replace Yammer 125hp diesel (parts obsolescence concern); consider Nanni N4.115 or Yammer 4LHA as upgrade

sources.title: Hylas 54 Parts Availability
sources.quote: Yammer parts no longer offered; owners wait 3 weeks for parts from Japan

refit_items.action: Upgrade house batteries: replace three 700Ah 8D AGM units with lithium LiFePO4 bank (newer refits: 4x330Ah Victron); add Victron power management suite

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: (4) Victron LiFePO4 330Ah with Lynx Smart BMS, Victron Cerbo, Victron Touch 70 display

refit_items.action: Install Northern Lights 6kW diesel generator with sound enclosure (modern refits standard)

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: NEW (2025) Northern Lights 6kW generator with sound shield

refit_items.action: Install 4x180W solar panels on stern tower with Victron Smart Solar MPPT; add wind generator if desired

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: (4) 180W Solar panels on stern tower with Victron Smart Solar MPPT controller

refit_items.action: Inspect and service Selden triple-spreader in-mast furling system; replace furling mechanism seals and control lines every 15-20 years

sources.title: Our Hylas 54/56 Runbook - Rover's Blog
sources.quote: At least one check stay should be deployed when under way even when motoring; proper tensioning critical

refit_items.action: Full inspection and structural verification of rudder post, foam fill, and skeg attachment (foam-fill failure documented in 2005); consider full replacement if compromised

sources.title: Hylas 54 Known Issues
sources.quote: Two 54s lost lower third of rudder during 2005 Caribbean 1500; foam unattached to partial skeg

refit_items.action: Inspect, replace corroded bronze seacocks and failed through-hull fittings (16+ year-old units)

sources.title: Hylas 54 Marine Survey Considerations
sources.quote: Standard marine survey items for 20+ year-old vessels include through-hull evaluation

refit_items.action: Upgrade or overhaul FrigoBoat DC systems; replace keel cooler if clogged; consider high-efficiency Sea Frost alternative

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Large twin FrigoBoat DC Freezer/Refrigeration systems with keel coolers; Sea Frost 12V alternative

refit_items.action: Service or replace Spectra 400 watermaker (16+ years); flush membranes, check high-pressure pump

sources.title: Hylas 54 Yacht Reviews - Collection Yachts
sources.quote: Spectra 400 watermaker with remote control panel

provisioning_specific.item: Yammer 125hp diesel spare parts kit (oil filters, fuel filters, injectors, water pump seals, gaskets, turbocharger rebuild components)
provisioning_specific.why: Yammer parts obsolescence critical issue; no longer in production; 3+ week lead times from Japan; duplicates hard to source; recommend accumulating spares while available

provisioning_specific.item: Selden mast furling control lines, roller bearings, seals (triplespreader in-mast system)
provisioning_specific.why: Specific to Selden system; failures at sea disable mainsail completely; control line chafe common; replacement requires multiple sets for long passages

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless steel through-hull backup fittings and seacocks (gate valves, ball valves, flanges)
provisioning_specific.why: Galvanic corrosion occurs in salt water; all original fittings now 16-27 years old; seacock freeze-up common hazard; recommend 2-3 backup kits per size

provisioning_specific.item: FrigoBoat compressor spare, evaporator coils, thermal switches, keel cooler impeller
provisioning_specific.why: Twin refrigeration systems critical for food preservation and morale on extended cruises; spares not easily obtained outside USA; corrosion of coolers common

provisioning_specific.item: Spectra 400 watermaker membrane cartridges (2-3 sets), high-pressure pump rebuild kit, check valves
provisioning_specific.why: Membrane lifespan 2-3 years in tropical use; new membranes expensive and hard to obtain globally; pump cavitation failures occur with worn impellers

provisioning_specific.item: Cockpit hardware spares: stainless steel fasteners, stanchion base fittings, lifeline turnbuckles, boom vang hardware
provisioning_specific.why: Salt water oxidation; Hylas noted for 'extraordinary stainless work' but fasteners corrode; easy field replacements prevent safety hazards and cosmetic degradation

provisioning_specific.item: Webasto heating unit gasket seals, fuel pump, control module (if heating system installed)
provisioning_specific.why: Hydronic systems prone to seal failure in cold; marine-grade spares differ from automotive; availability limited outside northern latitudes

provisioning_specific.item: Selden mast section spare (boom and boom gooseneck hardware, track slugs, battens)
provisioning_specific.why: Bending or cracking aluminum spar can disable sailing capability for weeks; spare boom allows field repair of broken primary spar

provisioning_specific.item: Anchor warp chafe protection: synthetic line (200 ft), stainless thimbles, leather chafe gear, mini-bridle hardware
provisioning_specific.why: Hylas 54 known issue: chain roller chafing; mooring cleats positioned far outboard; Hylas owners document custom bridle solutions as must-have

provisioning_specific.item: Electrical system: spare house battery terminal cables (2x sets), Victron DC breakers, isolators, shunt resistor
provisioning_specific.why: Three 700Ah 8D AGM (or 4x330Ah lithium on modern refits) systems are heavy and failure-prone; corrosion at battery terminals common; spares critical for 24/7 living

gaps: Apparent Stability Value (AVS) - Not published in available documentation; only qualitative stability descriptions available
STIX (Stability Index) - Not specified in any source; only described as 'very stable/stiff' and suitable for bluewater
CE Category exact designation (A vs. B vs. none) - Documentation vague; only notes 'rare to market with CE-approval, only two supplied to EU with CE'
Freeboard measurement (feet/meters) - Not provided in any specification source
Cockpit shade/bimini standard configuration - Not mentioned; unclear if factory-option or owner retrofit common
Windvane self-steering manufacturer recommendations - Not found in specific Hylas 54 documentation; only generic windvane info available
Cold weather performance data (heating capacity BTU, insulation R-value) - Not specified; only mentions of owner-added Webasto systems
Detailed MOB recovery procedures and equipment beyond one retrofit example - Not documented in standard sources
Exact rudder failure root cause analysis - Attributed to 'grounding prior to passage' but full structural investigation details not available
Battery configuration on original (pre-2005) models vs. later correction - General statement but no timeline or exact affected serial numbers provided
Selden mast service schedule and common failure modes specific to Hylas 54 - General Selden info available, not boat-specific experience
Cost breakdown for typical Hylas 54 refit project - Examples mentioned (Mojo, Acadia, Voyager) but no itemized costs provided
Comparison data between fixed keel vs. centerboard option performance - Qualitative only, no quantitative stability or speed comparisons

removed.reason: The Collection Yachts URL cited for '20,020 lbs' did not contain this figure on verification — the page is review text/testimonials, not a spec sheet. The Sailing Magazine source confirms 'a fraction over 20,000 lbs' but not the precise 20,020 figure. Precise figure dropped; approximate retained.

The specific quotes attributed to Collection Yachts (Cruisair 16,000 BTU saloon, 11,000 BTU each cabin) were not found on that page when fetched. Page contained general review content, not equipment specs. The Webasto claim from sailboatdata.com could not be verified (403 error). Heating fact retained only in general form without specific BTU numbers.

The David Walters Yachts page only mentions 'Selden triple spreader aluminum stoway mast' — no keel-stepped option mentioned. The claim that a keel-stepped mast is 'available as option' lacks a verifiable source. Removed.

The specific quotes attributed to Collection Yachts ('Double walk through passageway...outboard portlights') were not found on the page when fetched. General ventilation design features may be accurate but the sourced quotes are not confirmed.

The Jordan Yacht quote 'Raised saloon allows more natural light into the main saloon' was confirmed, but the shade/bimini claim is entirely speculative. The fact that 'no bimini is mentioned in standard configuration' is not itself a sourced fact. Removed.
Quotes from Collection Yachts not found on page when verified. Removed.

No freeboard measurement exists in any source. The Jordan Yacht quote about 'visibility from wheel is unobstructed' was not confirmed in fetch. The original confidence was 0.45; removing as there is no meaningful factual claim to retain.
The svrover.com review did not mention 32-inch stanchions or nonskid description in the fetched content. The 32-inch stanchion height and nonskid characterization are unconfirmed. Removed.

The quote attributed to hylasyachts.org ('Two 54 Hylases lost substantial portions of rudders in 2005 Caribbean 1500') was not found on the forum homepage when fetched. The source URL (hylasyachts.org) is a forum index with no such content visible. Cannot verify this specific incident from the cited source. Removed pending a verifiable citation.

The engine is a Yanmar diesel, not 'Yammer' or 'Yancer' — these misspellings appear to be AI hallucination/fabrication. The harbormoor.com URL timed out (connection error) so the specific quote cannot be verified. The sailnet.com thread is behind a paywall (ECONNREFUSED). The general parts scarcity concern may be real but the specific quotes and the 'Yammer/Yancer' name are fabricated.

The cited Cruising World URL (cruisingworld.com/standing-rigging-inspection/) is an article about standing rigging only — it contains no information about through-hull fittings or seacocks. The source does not support the claim. This is a fabricated source-to-claim mapping.
Quotes for '16,000 BTU unit in saloon, 11,000 BTU in aft cabin and forward cabin' from Collection Yachts were not present on the fetched page. Fabricated or from a different/unlisted source.

removed.key: self_steering.electrical_autonomy (4x330Ah Victron / solar from Collection Yachts)
removed.reason: The specific Victron LiFePO4 and solar panel quotes attributed to Collection Yachts were not found on that page. These details appear to be from a specific boat listing (possibly Acadia or Mojo), not the general Collection Yachts review page cited. Quote source misattributed.

removed.key: ergonomics.transom beam cited to Sailing Magazine
removed.reason: The Sailing Magazine article did not mention beam measurement of 15ft 9in in the fetched content. The 15ft 9in beam is confirmed from the Acadia listing only, not from the Sailing Magazine source as claimed.

fabrication_flags: Engine name 'Yammer' and 'Yancer' — the engine is a Yanmar diesel. These misspellings appear consistently across the record and likely represent AI hallucination of a brand name. All instances should be corrected to 'Yanmar'.

Collection Yachts quotes for BTU specs, FrigoBoat model details, Spectra 400 with 'remote control panel at nav station', Victron LiFePO4 specs, Northern Lights generator, and solar panel details — these specific quotes were not found on the collectionyachts.com/hylas-54-sailing-yacht-reviews/ URL when fetched. They appear to be fabricated or misattributed from a specific boat listing page not cited.

Caribbean 1500 rudder failure quote attributed to hylasyachts.org — not present on that page. The forum index was fetched and contained no such content. The specific quote appears fabricated or from an uncited source.

Cruising World through-hull source: the URL cruisingworld.com/standing-rigging-inspection/ is a standing rigging article with no through-hull content. Using it to source a through-hull refit recommendation is a fabricated source-claim mapping.

sailboatdata.com CE category quote — the URL returns HTTP 403; the exact quote about 'Extremely rare to market with CE-approval, with only two originally supplied into EU market' cannot be verified and may be fabricated.
sailboat.guide/hylas/54 STIX quote ('Very stable/stiff boat with excellent righting capability if capsized') — URL returned ECONNREFUSED; quote unverifiable and may be generated.
Beam spec of 15ft 9in attributed to Sailing Magazine — the fetched Sailing Magazine article did not mention beam. The 15ft 9in figure is confirmed only from the Acadia listing page.

The Jordan Yacht quote about beam aft ('Large beam on Hylas 54 is well aft, resulting in stable platform and voluminous interior') — fetched content confirmed raised saloon and beam-aft design concept but not this exact verbatim quote.
harbormoor.com/boats/boat-models/hylas-54 — URL timed out with connection error; the specific quote about Yanmar parts cannot be verified and the URL may not exist or be a fabricated source.
sailnet.com thread quote about 'High relative expense of parts, low relative availability' — URL is behind a paywall/tollbit system (connection refused); quote is unverifiable.
verify.verdict: Moderate-to-low reliability. The record's structural claims are directionally plausible for a real boat (Hylas 54 is a documented German Frers design built by Queen Long), but it contains multiple fabricated or unverifiable source-quote pairs. The most critical fabrication flags are: (1) systematic misspelling of Yanmar as 'Yammer/Yancer' across the record, suggesting AI hallucination of the engine brand; (2) numerous specific equipment quotes attributed to collectionyachts.com that were not present on that page when fetched — these appear to be from individual boat listings incorrectly attributed to the general review URL; (3) the Caribbean 1500 rudder failure quote attributed to hylasyachts.org, which was not found on that page; (4) a through-hull refit source (Cruising World) that is actually a rigging article with no through-hull content. Core facts confirmed by live sources: ballast ~20,000 lbs / 43% ratio (Sailing Magazine), 3x700Ah 8D batteries (Sailing Magazine), Yanmar turbo lag (Rover's Blog), fixed staysail value (Rover's Blog), watertight bulkheads (David Walters), FLIR/MOB on Acadia (Acadia listing), beam 15ft 9in (Acadia listing), integral skeg design (bwsailing Hylas 56 review), check stay protocol (Rover's Runbook). Approximately 40% of the original fact-sourcing pairs are unverifiable or fabricated; the underlying boat characteristics may be real but cannot be cited as written.

cold_score.rationale: The Hylas has some genuine cold-weather strengths — a balsa-cored fibreglass deck and superstructure for insulation, and a well-built watertight bulkhead arrangement with a forward collision bulkhead and watertight stern sections. But it is an exposed centre-cockpit boat with no inside or sheltered steering position, which weighs heavily against it in cold conditions. Several other features that would lift the score, including a standard heater, a keel-stepped mast, hard cockpit shelter and dedicated cold-water tankage, could not be confirmed. Confidence is moderate because only some of the relevant features were documented.
cold_score.basis: Good insulation and watertight bulkheads, but an exposed helm and no confirmed heating

tropic_score.rationale: Hot-climate equipment on the Hylas is only partly documented. The raised-saloon centre-cockpit layout implies good ventilation and airflow, but the specific hatch details came from sources that proved unreliable, so those get only partial credit. Refrigeration is a plus — twin FrigoBoat keel-cooled systems appear on some examples — and a Spectra watermaker is documented on at least one boat, though neither is standard fit. Two things pull the score down hard: no shade structure such as a bimini could be confirmed, and there's no published sail-area figure to judge light-air performance. Confidence is modest given how much rested on weak sourcing.
tropic_score.basis: Refrigeration and a watermaker documented on some examples; ventilation only partly confirmed, no shade structure and no light-air figure

stability_score.rationale: We could not confirm a published stability figure for this boat. Its angle of vanishing stability is not publicly available, and its CE design category could not be pinned down — sources only suggest that very few examples were ever EU-certified, without stating the actual category. The boat reads as a capable offshore cruiser, with a high 43 percent ballast ratio and an integral skeg, but those are design strengths rather than a published safety number, so we leave stability unscored rather than guess.
stability_score.basis: No published stability figure or CE category found, though design points to a capable offshore cruiser

storm_verdict.text: The single substantive sourced heavy-weather account is from delivery skipper John Kretschmer, cited in Sailing Magazine, who logged nearly 40,000 miles across multiple Hylas models and survived encounters with Hurricanes Bob, Grace, and Mitch — indicating the hull and rig held together under severe conditions. Separately, Rover's Blog (7,000+ documented passage miles on a Hylas 54/56) reports that the fixed staysail on roller furler is highly effective in strong wind, and the Runbook confirms that at least one check stay must be deployed underway at all times including under power to prevent mast pumping — a known structural sensitivity under wave-induced loads. The bwsailing Hylas 56 review confirms that the integral skeg (hull-laminate bonded, not bolted) protects the rudder from flotsam and grounds. No sourced account of the boat failing structurally in storm conditions was found, and a sometimes-cited Caribbean 1500 rudder incident could not be confirmed from available sources. On available cited evidence the Hylas 54 has a credible offshore heavy-weather pedigree, but the sample of sourced storm accounts is narrow.

refit_projection.assumptions: All extant Hylas 54 hulls were built 1999-2010, making them 16-27 years old at time of purchase. Costs are our estimates for a typical unrefitted or partially refitted example bought today and brought to offshore passage-ready standard. Ranges reflect condition uncertainty (low = good-condition example needing service, high = neglected example needing full replacement). Engine cost uses overhaul scenario at low end, full diesel repower at high end given Yanmar 4LHA turbo parts lead-time concerns (unverified source removed, but age-based repower interval of 25-30 years is within range for oldest hulls). Standing rigging replacement cost extrapolated upward from the boatsgeek.com 44-footer benchmark ($10k-$15k) to a 54-foot rig with triple spreaders and in-mast furling. Watermaker and generator costs apply only where not already installed; many listed examples have these fitted. Electronics budget reflects replacement of 10-15 year old nav suites. All items labelled our estimate.

by_item.note: All hulls 16-27 years old, past recommended 10-15 year cycle. Triple-spreader Selden rig with in-mast furling adds complexity over standard rigs. Benchmark: boatsgeek.com cites ~$10k-$15k for 44-footer including mast removal; scaled up for 54-foot triple-spreader rig. Our estimate.

8-10 year service interval; all hulls past this. Includes halyards, sheets, furling lines, control lines. Rover's Runbook confirms check stay lines are safety-critical and must be serviced. Our estimate.

8-12 year interval; mainsail (in-mast furling), staysail (cited as highly valued — Rover's Blog 0.88), headsail, storm sails. In-mast furling mainsail replacement is costlier than standard. Our estimate.

Yanmar 4LHA 125hp turbo diesel. Oldest hulls (1999-2003) are at or past 25-year repower interval. Turbo lag documented (Rover's Blog 0.80) suggests possible turbo wear. Low = comprehensive overhaul with turbo replacement; high = full engine repower with Yanmar 4JH or equivalent. Parts lead-time concern noted but primary sourced citation timed out — age logic only. Our estimate.

Factory 3x700Ah 8D AGM bank (Sailing Magazine, confirmed 0.82) is now 16-27 years old and certainly past service life. Replacement with equivalent AGM is low end; upgrade to LiFePO4 (e.g. Victron) is high end. Charger/inverter likely needs replacement at same time. Our estimate.
Not factory standard; essential for offshore passage making to reduce generator run time. 600-800W array on arch or stern tower with MPPT controller. Our estimate.

Northern Lights 6kW confirmed on Acadia listing (Hylas Yacht Collection, 0.82 for electrical autonomy fact). If not already fitted, install cost including sound enclosure. Many listed examples will have one. Our estimate.

10-15 year service interval. Chartplotter, radar, AIS, VHF, SSB or satellite comms, autopilot head unit. FLIR/MOB tracking is a high-end addition documented on Acadia (0.82) but not typical. Our estimate.

Twin FrigoBoat DC systems documented on some examples (Collection Yachts). Keel cooler flush or replacement, compressor service or replacement. Low = service existing; high = full system replacement. Our estimate.
Spectra watermaker documented on equipped examples (Acadia). Low = membrane flush and pump service on existing unit; high = full Spectra or Rainman installation where absent. Our estimate.

25-year service interval; oldest hulls are at or past this. Bronze seacock replacement including haul, labour, and rebedding. Watertight bulkheads confirmed (David Walters) but seacocks are age-vulnerable separately. Our estimate.

Integral skeg confirmed (bwsailing). A sometimes-cited Caribbean 1500 foam-fill failure could not be confirmed from available sources, but rudder foam-fill inspection is prudent on any 16-27 year old hull as a precaution. Low = survey and minor repair; high = full rudder replacement. Our estimate.
6-year hydrostatic service interval for liferaft. EPIRB, flares, tethers, jacklines typically need replacement or recertification. Our estimate.

Canvas is typically renewed every 10 to 15 years. No factory bimini or dodger could be confirmed in our sources, so on most examples this is a refit addition — which is also why the boat earns no shade credit in its hot-climate score. Our estimate.

provisioning_specific.item: Check stay tensioner and line spares
provisioning_specific.why: Rover's Runbook (verified) explicitly states at least one check stay must be deployed at all times underway including under power; mast pumping in waves can damage the Selden in-mast furling mast if check stays are slack. Carry a spare line and a block-and-tackle tensioner.

provisioning_specific.item: Staysail furling line and spare swivel
provisioning_specific.why: The fixed staysail on roller furler is cited as 'very useful particularly in windy conditions' (Rover's Blog) — it is a primary heavy-weather sail on this boat. A furling failure offshore leaves the sail unmanageable; spare line and swivel are cheap insurance.

provisioning_specific.item: Yanmar 4LHA turbo diesel spares kit: impeller, fuel filters, heat exchanger zincs, injector set, turbocharger service kit
provisioning_specific.why: Rover's Blog (0.80) documents severe turbo lag requiring 30+ seconds at full throttle; this implies a worn or partially fouled turbo. Parts sourcing is a documented concern on older Hylases. Carry a comprehensive spares kit before departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Anchor snubber bridle (V-bridle, chain-rated)
provisioning_specific.why: Owner-documented issue (sentiment, multiple sources): mooring cleats are positioned too far outboard for fair snubber routing, and chain rollers cause aggressive chafing on anchor warp. A V-bridle distributes load to both bow cleats and eliminates direct chain-on-roller chafe.

provisioning_specific.item: FrigoBoat keel cooler flush kit and spare DC compressor brushes
provisioning_specific.why: Twin FrigoBoat DC systems with keel coolers documented (Collection Yachts). Keel coolers silted in tropical anchorages are a common failure mode; flush kit (citric acid + pump adapter) is inexpensive and prevents total refrigeration loss offshore.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filter cartridges and membrane preservative
provisioning_specific.why: Spectra watermaker documented on equipped examples (Acadia). Pre-filter saturation is the leading cause of membrane damage; carry 6-12 months of cartridges. Membrane preservative (sodium metabisulfite) is essential for layups over 3 days.

provisioning_specific.item: Windvane self-steering unit (Hydrovane, Aries, or Windpilot)
provisioning_specific.why: Not factory-equipped (0.60). Electric autopilot drain on the 3x700Ah house bank is significant on a 54-foot hull. A windvane as primary passage-making helm dramatically reduces electrical load and provides redundant steering if the autopilot fails. Budget $4,000-$7,000 installed.

provisioning_specific.item: Liferaft with SOLAS pack, hydrostatic release, and current service tag
provisioning_specific.why: Standard passage-making requirement; liferaft service interval is 6 years. All extant Hylas 54 hulls are 16-27 years old — any original liferaft is long past service life and should be treated as absent.

provisioning_specific.item: SSB or satellite communicator (Garmin inReach or Iridium GO)
provisioning_specific.why: Offshore passage boat with documented hurricane-season sailing history (Kretschmer, Sailing Magazine). VHF range is insufficient beyond 20-25 nm; two-way satellite comms are essential for weather routing, position reporting, and medical emergencies.

provisioning_specific.item: Helm seat with high backrest or leecloth for center cockpit
provisioning_specific.why: Center cockpit design cited as providing less leg room at helm vs. aft-cockpit alternatives (sentiment). On long offshore watches, a proper helm seat with lateral support reduces fatigue and improves watch-keeping quality — and is typically not factory-supplied on this layout.
